original_hypothesis.title: CoreRef.emergencyAction performs arbitrary external calls without per-call target allowlisting; a compromised GOV role can sweep any approvals/ETH held by protocol contracts
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: CoreRef.emergencyAction is a governor-only arbitrary call primitive that can call any target with arbitrary calldata and ETH value. Many protocol contracts (including those that may hold tokens/ETH or have token approvals) inherit CoreRef. If the GOVERNOR role is compromised, the attacker can use emergencyAction from any such contract to execute token transfers, manipulate approvals, or interact with external protocols in a way not anticipated by individual contract access controls.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is not a bug per se but a high-impact threat hypothesis to validate in Phase D: confirm which deployed contracts expose emergencyAction and what assets/approvals they hold (e.g., SurplusGuildMinter approving ProfitManager, LendingTerms holding collateral, ProfitManager holding CREDIT). Quantify blast radius and whether any contracts unintentionally hold spendable ETH/ERC20 approvals that make this materially worse than normal governor powers.

proof_of_signal.pattern: governor-only arbitrary target.call{value}(callData) loop; no allowlist; no reentrancy guard; forwards ETH

preconditions: Attacker controls GOVERNOR role (key compromise or governance takeover)
Target CoreRef-derived contract holds ERC20/ETH balances or has allowances set

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: arbitrary call primitive with full execution power
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: protocol-wide asset sweep and configuration tampering if governor is compromised
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no timelock enforcement at this layer; no target/function restrictions

analysis_questions: Which deployed contracts expose emergencyAction and what token/ETH balances do they hold in steady state?
Do any contracts approve third parties (e.g., SurplusGuildMinter approves ProfitManager) that could be redirected via emergencyAction?
Is emergencyAction itself only reachable through a timelock-controlled GOVERNOR role in production?
validation_requirements: Enumerate approvals and balances on CoreRef-derived contracts
Confirm GOVERNOR is held by timelock and vetoed appropriately in deployment

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: With GOVERNOR compromised, attacker calls emergencyAction on ProfitManager to transfer out all CREDIT holdings (surplus buffers) or on LendingTerms to transfer out collateral tokens, bypassing normal lifecycle functions.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Total loss of protocol-controlled assets (collateral escrow, buffers, peg reserves if held by CoreRef-derived contracts with callable paths)

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitor emergencyAction calls/events (CoreUpdate emitted elsewhere; emergencyAction has no event)

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
